problem_addressed: Limited availability of annotated medical imaging data and the ineffectiveness of transfer learning from natural image domains to medical tasks.
core_claim: A lightweight architecture (MAKNet) trained with semi-supervised pseudo-labels achieves superior performance with 60-70% fewer parameters compared to popular architectures.
key_contributions: Proposed MAKNet with mixed asymmetric kernels (MAKConv) for efficient feature extraction
Semi-supervised learning pipeline using teacher-student framework to generate pseudo-labels from large unlabeled medical dataset
Demonstrated importance of domain-specific knowledge for effective transfer learning in medical imaging
novelty: MAKNet introduces mixed asymmetric kernels (k×k, 1×k, k×1, 1×1) in a single conv layer to capture multi-dimensional features efficiently, combined with semi-supervised pseudo-labeling for domain-specific transfer learning.
method_summary: MAKNet uses dense blocks with MAKConv layers, followed by standard conv, CBAM attention, and Mish activation. Semi-supervised training employs a teacher network (15 epochs) to generate pseudo-labels (>0.5 confidence) for unlabeled data, training a student network on concatenated labeled/unlabeled batches.
method_components: MAKConv: Mixed asymmetric kernels (spatial, single-dimension, depthwise)
Dense Blocks: 3-6 connected MAKConv layers with standard conv + CBAM
Semi-supervised Pipeline: Teacher-student framework with pseudo-label generation
Preprocessing: HU normalization, background clipping, 3-slice input channels
key_innovation: Mixed asymmetric kernels (MAKConv) enable comprehensive multi-dimensional feature extraction with significantly reduced parameters, while semi-supervised pseudo-labeling leverages large unlabeled medical datasets for domain-specific knowledge transfer.
assumptions: Unlabeled medical dataset contains sufficient diversity for meaningful pseudo-labels
Confidence threshold (>0.5) and ontology filtering produce reliable pseudo-labels
Domain-specific features transfer better than natural image features
theoretical_grounding: No explicit theoretical proofs or mathematical derivations provided; empirical validation through comparative experiments.
hyperparameters: VGG16 trained on TCIA for 5 epochs; LesaNet fine-tuned on DeepLesion with learning rate 0.001 for 15 epochs
headline_result: 0.9403 AUC on hand-labeled test set for LesaNet (TCIA) on DeepLesion dataset
improvement_over_baseline: TCIA pretraining achieves 0.0005 higher AUC than ImageNet on hand-labeled test set
stated_limitations: Knowledge transfer across disparate domains is less effective
unstated_weaknesses: No thorough comparison with other lightweight architectures
Doesn't discuss how pseudo-labels are generated or their quality
No mention of how the method handles label noise
No information on how the method scales with dataset size
threats_to_validity: No information on cross-validation or result validation
Unclear if domain-specific knowledge transfer was tested across multiple domains
No discussion of potential overfitting despite lightweight architecture
Unclear if pseudo-labels were validated for quality
open_questions: How are pseudo-labels generated and validated?
What is the exact architecture of MAKNet?
How does the method perform under different levels of label noise?
How does the method scale with dataset size?
future_work: Developing more sophisticated domain adaptation techniques
Improving pseudo-label generation and validation methods
gaps_this_enables: Research on lightweight architectures for medical imaging
Need for better methods of generating and validating pseudo-labels
Focus on domain-specific knowledge in transfer learning
Research on trade-offs between model size and performance in medical imaging
relevance_to_topic: This paper is relevant as it addresses semi-supervised learning for medical imaging, which is a core component of the project, though it doesn't specifically address uncertainty-aware pseudo-label selection or label noise.
best_result: 0.9403 AUC on hand-labeled test set for LesaNet (TCIA) on DeepLesion dataset
limitations_stated: Knowledge transfer across disparate domains is less effective
title: Domain-Specific, Semi-Supervised Transfer Learning for Medical Imaging
